Another good example of loss framing comes from the high end audio technology manufacturer Bose Acoustic a few years ago. Bose was very enthusiastic about its new home audio system that they knew would outperform its competition. Their initial advertising campaign ads began new, followed by introducing the new Bose Wave music system, and then that went on to detail all of its desirable features. But sales from the ads proved very disappointing to Bose, so they called me in to redesign the ads. After looking at the ad copy, I told them, I'm going to recommend against revising the whole message. This is a good ad for Bose customers, who want to know all of the details regarding the new features, simplicity, and convenience of the system. They're not impulsive, spur-of-the-moment kind of buyers. They want to know about these benefits before they decide. But I am going to recommend one change, to focus them on the idea of losing those benefits, by changing the wording at the top of the ad from new to hear what you've been missing. As a result, sales increased by 45%. Why? Because now those benefits, the new features, the new simplicity, and convenience, stood to be lost, not gained. Because sometimes combining more than one of the principles magnifies the total effect, I also recommended that Bose include testimonials from experts in the fields of audition and technology, who by then had said favorable things about the new system that could be collected and inserted into the ad. When Bose did that, thereby adding the force of the principle of authority, sales increased by 60% compared to the initial ad. This is a good example of how we can effectively and ethically bundle multiple principles into an appeal, provided they naturally coexist in that situation.
